primary_diagnosis.name: Acute ischaemic stroke (suspected left MCA territory, likely cardioembolic)

pathophysiology_summary: In a patient with known atrial fibrillation who recently stopped anticoagulation, a cardiac thrombus (typically from the left atrial appendage) has likely embolised to the left middle cerebral artery territory, producing the classic FAST triad: sudden right-sided facial droop, right hemiparesis (arm and leg), and severe expressive aphasia — all localising to the left hemisphere, consistent with left MCA territory ischaemia. Time is the dominant determinant of outcome: intravenous thrombolysis is generally time-limited to within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, and endovascular thrombectomy may be considered up to 24 hours in selected patients with confirmed large vessel occlusion — both requiring urgent hospital-based imaging (CT/MRI) to first exclude haemorrhage. Establishing the precise 'last known well' time is therefore one of the single most important pieces of information the telehealth assessment can obtain. Dysphagia is common after stroke and carries a high aspiration risk, so strict nil-by-mouth is essential pending formal swallowing assessment. Hypoglycaemia is an important stroke mimic that must be considered, though it cannot be tested via telehealth and should not delay stroke-code activation.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden facial droop, arm weakness, and speech difficulty — the classic FAST triad.
A recognised and reversible stroke mimic that must be screened for, though it cannot be excluded via telehealth and should not delay the stroke-code call.

differential.diagnosis: Todd's paresis (post-ictal, following an unwitnessed seizure)
differential.evidence_basis: Can mimic focal neurological deficits, though the absence of any witnessed seizure activity and the sudden, non-fluctuating onset favour a vascular cause.

Bell's palsy causes a complete unilateral facial droop involving the forehead and does not cause limb weakness or aphasia, both of which are present here, indicating a central rather than peripheral facial nerve lesion.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The classic FAST triad should already place acute stroke as leading from the opening complaint; hypoglycaemia must be mentioned as a mimic but should not delay emergency escalation via telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Acute ischaemic stroke (suspected left MCA, likely cardioembolic)
differential.evidence_basis: Facial droop (DI-001), hemiparesis (DI-002), aphasia (DI-003), a clear last-known-well time within the thrombolysis window (DI-004), and known AF with recently ceased anticoagulation (DI-005) together confirm a high-probability cardioembolic ischaemic stroke requiring emergency stroke-code activation.
No witnessed seizure activity and a sudden, sustained (rather than gradually resolving post-ictal) deficit argue against this.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage acute stroke should be the sole leading diagnosis, with the AI Doctor's task being to establish the precise onset time and activate a stroke code immediately, not to further refine the differential via telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Acute ischaemic stroke (suspected left MCA territory, likely cardioembolic)
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context; confirmation requires urgent CT/MRI brain to exclude haemorrhage and characterise the infarct, unavailable remotely. Immediate stroke-code emergency escalation is the only appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A management plan without immediate 000 stroke-code activation and an explicit last-known-well time at this stage represents a critical, time-costly failure given the strict thrombolysis window.

red_flags.description: Sudden unilateral (right-sided) facial droop, visible on video
red_flags.clinical_significance: Core FAST criterion; video-observable without requiring physical examination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Stroke not recognised in time; thrombolysis/thrombectomy window missed.

red_flags.description: Sudden unilateral (right-sided) arm and leg weakness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Core FAST criterion, localising to the contralateral (left) hemisphere.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Severity under-estimated; time-critical treatment window lost.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Core FAST criterion, strongly localising to the dominant (typically left) hemisphere.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Language deficit misattributed to confusion rather than a stroke.

red_flags.description: Clear, precise last-known-well time (approximately 45 minutes prior)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The single most critical piece of information for determining eligibility for time-limited reperfusion therapies (thrombolysis, thrombectomy).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Uncertain onset time could exclude the patient from thrombolysis even if he would otherwise be eligible.

red_flags.description: Power 0/5 right upper limb, 1/5 right lower limb, leftward gaze deviation, irregularly irregular pulse on examination (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms the severity and localisation of the deficit and the likely cardioembolic source; requires formal neurological and cardiovascular examination unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — escalation must rely on the video-observable and historical FAST features above.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Delaying stroke-code activation to obtain a blood glucose reading
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: While hypoglycaemia is an important stroke mimic, the AI Doctor cannot measure blood glucose via telehealth chat; this must not be used as a reason to delay calling 000 and activating a stroke code — the emergency team will check glucose on arrival.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to establish a precise last-known-well time
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Given the strict time windows for thrombolysis and thrombectomy, failing to specifically ask for and communicate the precise onset/last-known-well time to emergency services is a critical omission that could cost the patient eligibility for time-limited reperfusion therapy.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal neurological examination and NIHSS scoring
CT/MRI brain to exclude haemorrhage and characterise the infarct
Cardiac auscultation to confirm the irregular rhythm
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Emergency ambulance (000), Priority 1 dispatch with a pre-hospital stroke code directly to a comprehensive stroke centre
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Any delay — attempting further chat-based assessment, waiting to see if symptoms improve, or failing to establish and communicate the precise onset time — risks the patient falling outside the window for thrombolysis or thrombectomy, resulting in permanent, preventable neurological disability.
